method: First-order sign Markov P(next|cur) with add-k smoothing vs sign-unigram baseline; held-out cross-entropy (bits/sign) under 5-fold random CV and site-blocked leave-one-site-out; order-shuffle permutation null (R=200). Signs are ANONYMOUS ids (L2/L3).

robustness_checks.note: Frozen verdict uses the preregistered asymmetric cross-entropy (Markov predicts L+1 positions incl EOS, unigram predicts L). Verified the negative result is NOT a definitional artifact:
robustness_checks.interpretation: Under a symmetric definition (same predicted positions for both models) LA improvement is still negative (-0.16); restricted to len>=2 words it is still negative (-0.74). LB under the same symmetric def is +0.67. So the machinery detects redundancy in a real language (LB) and LA genuinely lacks generalizable first-order sign-sequential structure.

key_findings: PC PASSED: on LB (a real language) the sign-Markov beats the unigram by +0.260 bits/sign (p=0.0); false-positive rate on order-shuffled LA words is 0/20. Machinery is informative.

LA global 5-fold CV: first-order sign Markov held-out cross-entropy is 7.576 bits/sign vs unigram 6.577 bits/sign -> improvement = -0.999 bits/sign (Markov WORSE than unigram). The Markov overfits LA's transitions and does not generalize.
Estimated LA entropy rate (Markov held-out xent) = 7.576 bits/sign; LA unigram entropy = 6.577 bits/sign. For comparison LB entropy rate = 5.420 bits/sign (lower = more predictable).
Site-blocked leave-one-site-out: of 10 sites with >=30 words, only 1 (Iouktas, +0.326) shows Markov>unigram; 9/10 are negative. Sequential redundancy does NOT generalize across sites.

Robustness: the negative result holds under a symmetric cross-entropy definition (-0.16) and when restricted to length->=2 words (-0.74). LB under the same symmetric definition is +0.67. The contrast LA-negative vs LB-positive is real and large, not an artifact.

Structural reason: 56.5% of LA words are single signs (len==1), mean word length 1.84, vocab 259 signs -> very few transition observations per context, so a first-order model cannot learn generalizable sequential dependencies.
successor_hypotheses: H1: Test HIGHER-ORDER or sparse-prior Markov models (e.g. interpolated Kneser-Ney, or a Dirichlet prior on transitions) that may extract weak sequential structure the add-k first-order model misses.

H2: Test whether redundancy appears at the WORD level (word-token n-grams across an inscription) rather than the sign-within-word level -- LA may be sequentially structured between words, not within them.
H3: Test sign BIGRAM frequency-spectrum / Zipf-like statistics to see if a small set of frequent sign-pairs carries structure even when the full transition matrix does not generalize.
H4: Restrict to length->=3 words only (rare longer LA words) and re-test sequential redundancy there; if present, structure may be concentrated in a long-word minority.
H5: Compare LA entropy rate to a matched-random sign-sequence null (same unigram marginals, iid draws) to bound how far LA is from pure unigram output.

H6: Cross-script transfer test: train sign-Markov on LB, evaluate on LA held-out -- if LA xent under an LB-trained model is near its own unigram, that independently confirms no shared sequential regime.
H7: Test positional/templatic structure (sign-position-within-word distributions) instead of Markovian transition structure -- LA may be positionally structured rather than transitionally.
non_circular: Signs are ANONYMOUS identifiers throughout. No phonetic values, sound, meaning, reading, or language identification was used or inferred. LB was used ONLY as a positive-control benchmark (a known language to confirm the machinery detects redundancy). The redundancy metric is purely information-theoretic (held-out cross-entropy of anonymous sign sequences). A negative result means LA sign-within-word sequences lack generalizable first-order sequential structure; it is NOT a claim that LA is/is not a language.
deviations: No deviation from the frozen protocol. The frozen (asymmetric) cross-entropy definition was used for the verdict exactly as preregistered.

Transparency note (not a deviation): the frozen definition gives the Markov an extra EOS-prediction term per word (L+1 predicted positions vs L for the unigram), which inflates the Markov's per-sign cost especially for the 56.5% of LA words that are single signs. I verified post-hoc that the negative verdict is NOT an artifact of this asymmetry: under a symmetric definition LA improvement is still -0.16 bits/sign, and restricted to length->=2 words still -0.74. LB under the same symmetric definition is +0.67. The verdict (NEAR_RANDOM) is robust to the definition.
